title: Governing from Kodanad?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The printed source confirms the terminal date as 27 December 2013, which is missing from the OCR-derived chapter. It also restores several visibly damaged words and a short omitted phrase in the derived Tamil reading copy. The canonical source layer has not been altered.
paragraphs: The ninth resolution adopted at the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam General Council meeting held on 15 December 2013 was titled, “Implement the crop insurance scheme; provide relief for foot-and-mouth disease.” In that resolution, we described the farmers' dire condition. In particular, we said:

“Anticipating that last year's samba cultivation would be affected, the government took steps to implement the crop insurance scheme. Farmers' crops were insured through the Agriculture Department, Revenue Department and district administration. The government itself paid the farmers' contribution. This year, however, the Agriculture Department has not created any awareness at all about crop insurance among farmers engaged in cultivation. This General Council urges the Tamil Nadu Agriculture Department, at least now, to encourage farmers who have suffered repeatedly and are in despair, and to come forward to insure their crops without permitting any irregularities.”

But the Tamil Nadu government does not appear to have shown any concern for this request so far. The same resolution called for compensation to coconut farmers and urged the Tamil Nadu government immediately to compensate farmers who had lost cattle to foot-and-mouth disease, as the Karnataka government does. That appeal, too, does not seem to have reached this government's ears.

During the DMK government's tenure, paragraph 10 of the 2007-2008 Budget itself stated: “Beginning this year, as a special scheme for farmers, financial assistance will be provided in the coming financial year to enable one million farmers to obtain crop insurance, protecting them against crop losses caused by drought, floods and similar events. Fifteen crore rupees has been allocated for this purpose.”

Again, in paragraph 5 of the 2010-2011 Budget, we stated: “A scheme under which the government provides a 50 per cent subsidy towards the premium for crop insurance that compensates farmers affected by natural calamities has been implemented, enabling a larger number of farmers to benefit. Consequently, the number of insured farmers, which was 100,000 in 2005-2006, rose to 780,000 in 2009-2010. Over the past four years under the DMK government, Rs. 891 crore has been paid as compensation under this scheme. This Budget has also allocated Rs. 40 crore towards the premium subsidy and Rs. 200 crore as the Tamil Nadu government's share of compensation.”

When the AIADMK assumed office in 2011-2012, today's Finance Minister O. Panneerselvam read in the Assembly from that government's Budget: “To protect farmers from the effects of climatic changes, the National Agricultural Insurance Scheme will be implemented vigorously in every district. The present number of 800,000 farmers benefiting from the scheme will be doubled during the current year.”
Under the AIADMK government in 2005-2006, only 100,000 farmers had been insured. Under the DMK government, we raised that number to 780,000 in 2009-2010 and 818,000 in 2010-2011.
It was this figure that O. Panneerselvam told the Assembly in the 2011 Budget would be doubled, meaning it would be raised to 1.6 million farmers. But what actually happened?

They disclosed that themselves in the 2012-2013 Budget. Paragraph 32 says: “It is noteworthy that the number of farmers covered by crop insurance rose from 818,000 in 2010-2011 to 848,000 in 2011-2012.” They said in the Budget that the number would be raised to 1.6 million, but the actual increase was only 30,000!

Under the DMK government, Rs. 40 crore was allocated for the premium subsidy in 2009-2010. By contrast, page 11 of the AIADMK government's 2012-2013 policy note says that Rs. 30 crore was allocated as a premium subsidy and approximately 532,000 farmers were enrolled. This makes it easy to understand not only that the premium subsidy fell by Rs. 10 crore, but also that the number of beneficiaries declined from 818,000 under the DMK government to 532,000 under last year's AIADMK rule. The AIADMK ministers and the Chief Minister, who is conducting government business from Kodanad, must answer for the extent to which crop-insurance assistance has been provided to farmers under this government!

I had drawn attention to this crop-insurance assistance for farmers as early as last November. At the time, reports said that the Tamil Nadu Agriculture Department was neglecting the Central government's crop insurance scheme. After that report appeared, I said that farmers engaged in samba cultivation had been put to great hardship because the northeast monsoon had failed. Last year, owing to the absence of proper water inflow, neither kuruvai nor samba cultivation took place. This year, too, the kuruvai crop failed. It was in these circumstances that samba cultivation had begun. Yet the Agriculture Department had reportedly created no awareness whatsoever about crop insurance among this year's cultivators. I urged the Tamil Nadu Agriculture Department, at least now, to encourage farmers who have suffered repeatedly and fallen into despair, and to come forward to insure their crops.

Following me, leaders of several other political parties also made appeals on this issue. Even a resolution adopted at the Nagapattinam district DMK meeting, reported in today's *Murasoli*, says: “Farmers who insured their crops last year have suffered because the crop-insurance compensation was not paid promptly. With neither the Central government nor the insurance company having paid the compensation due to these farmers, this executive committee condemns the Tamil Nadu government for failing to pay its own share and demands that it do so immediately.” Beyond making this demand, they also held a demonstration against the Tamil Nadu government in front of the Nagapattinam District Collector's office, led by Member of Parliament and district DMK secretary Thambi A.K.S. Vijayan.

Likewise, Thambi Suba. Thangavelan, Ramanathapuram district DMK secretary and Member of the Legislative Assembly, said at a public meeting explaining the General Council resolutions: “Last year, Rs. 144 crore was allocated for crop insurance in Ramanathapuram district. Three months have passed since the Central government and the insurance company paid their shares. But the AIADMK government is delaying payment of its share. It must pay immediately and take steps to ensure that farmers receive their crop-insurance compensation.”

This problem, therefore, is not confined to one or two districts. It has become a major problem for farmers throughout Tamil Nadu. Yet the AIADMK government, which ought to act immediately, continues to turn a deaf ear. While this is the state of farmers' problems, dairy farmers have announced a one-month deadline and said they will agitate because the increase in the milk procurement price is inadequate. Sugarcane farmers have likewise raised their voices, saying that the procurement price fixed by the government is far too low and must be announced at Rs. 3,500 per tonne.

There is no need even to ask about the drinking-water problem. The flow of Krishna river water has reportedly stopped. Madurai district is said to be facing an unprecedented drought. Production has reportedly been affected at both the Mettur thermal power station and the Vallur power plant. Nothing need be said about law and order. Not a day passes without murders and robberies. Foot-and-mouth disease continues to claim cattle. Those who have lost their livestock wait in anguish, wondering when the government will ever provide relief. Amid all this, the AIADMK is apparently to hold public meetings explaining its General Council resolutions, but the party leader will not attend! How are they going to distribute “Amma's” family financial assistance to those who die each day?

Fishermen began a hunger strike in Nagapattinam. As soon as I heard of it, I sent the DMK Parliamentary Party leader, Thambi T.R. Baalu, and had them end the hunger strike. Though they ended the fast, they continue their strike from work. The fishermen's problem remains unresolved. It has been many months since the Central government asked the Tamil Nadu government to fix a date for representatives of Sri Lankan fishermen and Tamil Nadu fishermen to hold talks. When will the Tamil Nadu government conduct that meeting? And the Chief Minister, who ought to find solutions to all these problems, is apparently going to administer the government from Kodanad!
